query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
I now have comprehensive information to provide a thorough answer. Let me compile the complete response covering all the requirements and fees for submitting a valid large generator interconnection application in ISO New England.

# Requirements and Fees for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the latest **ISO New England Interconnection Process Guide** and **Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** of the ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT), a "large generator" generally refers to a generating facility **larger than 20 MW** interconnecting to the Administered Transmission System (Pool Transmission Facilities/PTF and Non-PTF). The LGIP applies to all Large Generating Facilities, except as expressly provided. Below is a consolidated overview of the requirements and fees to submit a valid Interconnection Request (IR) under the post-Order No. 2023 framework now in effect.

## 1. Eligibility and Path to the Application
Before submitting, the developer must confirm that the ISO-NE interconnection process (not a state process) applies, and obtain:
- An **ISO Customer ID** (via the "Request Generation Interconnect Customer ID" form submitted to memcoord@iso-ne.com) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide, 2026-05 filing window context].
- **Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance** for IR data.
- An **IRTT (Interconnection Request Tracking Tool) account**.
- Confirmation of the interconnection path (PTF vs. Non-PTF) and the interconnecting transmission owner (ITO) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; Interconnection Process Presentation, Feb 16, 2023].

The LGIP itself (Section 2.1) states that "the LGIP and LGIA shall apply to Interconnection Requests pertaining to Large Generating Facilities."

## 2. Timing: Submissions Only During a Cluster Request Window
Per FERC Order No. 2023, the LGIP operates on a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**. Applications are accepted **only during an open Cluster Request Window (CRW)**, expected to last **45 calendar days**. The next window was posted as **October 5, 2026 – November 19, 2026** (for the next Cluster Study) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; ER24-2009-001 (Aug 28, 2025)].

## 3. Required Contents of a Valid Interconnection Request (Section 3.4)
The Appendix 1 IR form (the official Interconnection Request form under the LGIP) must include the following items during the CRW, as specified in Section 3.4.2 and the Appendix 1 form [source: Appendix 1 to Schedule 22, ISO-NE; "Notice Regarding Regulatory Limitations and Site Control," June 2, 2025; ISO-NE Q&A on Order 2023]:

(a) **Completed Appendix 1 IR Form** – including:
  - Project information (name, type – new facility, uprate/Material Modification, market entry, or upgrade from NR to CNR).
  - Facility location (Town/City, County, State).
  - **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)** – a definitive POI is required.
  - Type of facility, fuel type, MW capacity (gross, net, and at the POI at multiple temperature points).
  - Whether the facility includes **electric storage**; if so, charging capability, aggregate reactive capability, and primary frequency response operating range.
  - General equipment configuration (# of units, GSUs, control technologies).
  - Requested **Commercial Operation Date**, **Initial Synchronization Date**, and **In-Service Date**.
  - Customer information and ISO Customer ID.

(b) **Type of Interconnection Service elected (one only)**:
  - **Network Resource Interconnection Service (NR/NI)** – energy only, OR
  - **Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR/CNI)** – energy and capacity.

(c) **Technical data and models** (per the IR Technical Data Submittal Guidance, Rev. 1.0 dated 07/21/2025), including:
  - PSS®E steady-state (.idv/.raw) and dynamic (.dyr/.snp) models.
  - Short-circuit data for ASPEN (.olr/.chf) with positive- and zero-sequence impedances.
  - PSCAD models (.pscx/.pswx) for inverter-based resources (compatible with Intel Fortran 12 or later, ≥10–20 μs time step).
  - PSS®E–PSCAD benchmarking report.
  - Project one-line diagram (stamped by a PE if > 50 kW).
  - PQ (D) curve (0.95 leading/lagging reactive capability).
  - Collection system detail impedance sheet.
  - PRC-024-2 or ISO-NE SRD voltage/frequency protection settings (Attachment A-1).
  - Generation/transformer data, Attachment A (supplementary wind/inverter data), Attachment A-1 (protection settings), and any Attachment 2 (Surplus Interconnection) materials if applicable [source: Interconnection Request Submission Job Aid, 1/12/2022; Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance, 7/21/2025].

(d) **Site Control** (Section 3.4.2) – the most significant non-financial eligibility hurdle:
  - **For CNR/CNI Service: 100% exclusive Site Control is required** – no exception.
  - **For NR/NI Service: 100% exclusive Site Control is required**, **UNLESS** a Regulatory Limitation applies. If so, the customer must submit:
    - A **$10,000/MW cash deposit** (minimum $500,000, maximum $2,000,000),
    - A signed affidavit from a corporate officer that Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations, and
    - Documentation sufficiently describing the source and effects of the Regulatory Limitation.
  - The customer must demonstrate 100% Site Control within 180 calendar days of the effective date of the executed Interconnection Agreement [source: Appendix 1 to Schedule 22; ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice, "Providing Proof of Regulatory Limitations As a Demonstration of Site Control," 6/2/2025].
  - Existing facility modifications (no additional real property) may self-certify Site Control.

(e) **Initial Deposit** – **$50,000** (potentially non-refundable), applied to ISO-NE costs of processing the IR, the study agreements and attachments, and the LGIA [source: LGIP Section 3.4.1.1, as finalized in the 5/3/2025 effective date filing (ER25-2149-000) and the January 23, 2024 NEPOOL TC presentation].

(f) **Study Deposit** – **$250,000** for a new request for NR or CNR Interconnection Service, or **$100,000** for a request to change from existing NR to CNR Interconnection Service. This deposit is applied toward the cost of the Cluster Study [source: LGIP Section 3.4.1.1].

(g) **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** – equal to **two times the study deposit** (i.e., **$500,000** for new NR/CNR IRs, or **$200,000** for NR-to-CNR upgrades), provided as either (i) cash by electronic transfer, (ii) an irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution (per the "List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers" on ISO-NE's public website), or (iii) a combination. (Per ISO-NE's December 21, 2023 TC summary, the Surety Bond Form is also accepted per the Order 2023 Key Project page; sureties must be on the List of Approved Surety Issuers.) This deposit may be refunded/credited per Section 3.7 depending on whether the customer advances or withdraws [source: LGIP Section 3.4.2(vi); LGIP Section 3.1 (Letter of Credit provisions); ISO-NE Order 2023 Key Project page – CRD Instructions/Forms].

The ISO must validate or notify of deficiencies within **5 business days** of receipt; the customer then has up to **10 business days** to cure (but no later than CRW close) [source: ISO-NE New Process FAQ, 1/18/2024, Q3; LGIP Section 3.4.4].

## 4. Post-Acceptance Financial Milestones (Beyond the IR Submission)
The fees above are those to submit a *valid* application. Additional financial commitments come into play during the cluster study cycle and beyond (these are part of the overall "requirements to remain in the process" but generally not part of the original IR submission package):

- **Additional CRD after Cluster Study Report**: an additional CRD equal to **5% of the customer's share of Network Upgrade costs** is due within 20 calendar days after the Cluster Study Report meeting.
- **Interconnection Facilities Study (Section 8)**: To enter the Facilities Study, a Commercial Readiness Deposit equal to the **greater of 25% of the estimated Facilities Study cost or $250,000** is required.
- **LGIA deposit (Section 11.3)**: Upon execution of the LGIA, an LGIA deposit is required (20% of Network Upgrade cost less prior readiness deposits on hand) [source: ISO-NE October 17, 2023 TC presentation, "Additional Information on the Cluster Study Cycle"; Appendix 3 to Schedule 22 (Facilities Study Agreement); IR Submittal guidance].

## 5. Other Submission Logistics
- **Form of IR**: Submit via IRTT during the CRW.
- **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)**: Within 15 business days after CRW close, ISO issues NDAs; all ICs must execute them before the Cluster Study Scoping Meeting.
- **Cluster Study Agreement (Appendix 2)**: Issued by ISO within 5 business days after CRW close; must be executed (along with all required technical data) prior to the close of the 60-calendar-day Customer Engagement Window.
- **Withdrawal penalties** apply per Section 3.7; deposits may be forfeited or refunded depending on when and why the customer withdraws [source: LGIP Section 3.7; ISO-NE Order 2023 implementation presentations].

## Summary of Fees to Submit a Valid Large Generator IR
| Fee / Deposit | Amount | Refundability | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial (Application) Deposit | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | ISO-NE costs of processing the IR, study agreements, LGIA development |
| Study Deposit | **$250,000** (new NR/CNR); $100,000 (NR→CNR change) | Refundable; applied to Cluster Study | Cluster Study costs |
| Commercial Readiness Deposit | **$500,000** (new; = 2× study deposit); $200,000 (NR→CNR change) | Refundable/creditable per Section 3.7; may be cash, LOC, or Surety Bond | Demonstrates commercial commitment |
| Site Control (NR only, with Regulatory Limitation) | **$10,000/MW** cash (min $500,000 / max $2,000,000) | Refundable upon demonstrated 100% Site Control | In lieu of Site Control for NR service when a Regulatory Limitation applies |
| Site Control (CNR) | 100% exclusive Site Control required | n/a | Eligibility – no deposit alternative |

## Sources
- ISO New England, **Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)**, effective 5/3/2025 (ER25-2149-000): https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf
- ISO New England, **Appendix 1 – Interconnection Request (LGIP)**: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf
- ISO New England, **Appendix 3 – Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement (LGIP)**: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf
- ISO New England, **Interconnection Process Guide**: https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide
- ISO New England, **"Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window"**: https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request
- ISO New England, **Interconnection Technical Notice – "Providing Proof of Regulatory Limitations As a Demonstration of Site Control"** (6/2/2025): https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf
- ISO New England, **Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance**, Rev. 1.0, 7/21/2025: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100026/interconnection-request-technical-data-submittal-guidance.pdf
- ISO New England, **Interconnection Request Submission Job Aid**, 1/12/2022: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/2022/01/interconnection-request-submission-job-aid.pdf
- ISO New England, **Planning Procedure No. 5-6 – Interconnection Planning Procedure for Generation and ETUs** (effective 10/11/2025): https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/rules_proceds/isone_plan/pp05_6/pp5_6.pdf
- ISO New England, **Order No. 2023 Key Project page** (CRD forms, LOCs, Surety issuers): https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project
- ISO New England, **Order 2023 Compliance Overview** – NEPOOL TC, 9/27/2023: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf
- ISO New England, **Tariff Redlines Summary** – NEPOOL TC, 1/23/2024 (LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 deposit amounts): https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf
- ISO New England, **Acceptable Form of Readiness Deposits** – NEPOOL TC, 12/21/2023: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf
- ISO New England, **New Process Related Questions** – Q&A document, 1/18/2024: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf
- ISO New England, **Transition Related Questions** – Q&A document, 12/15/2023: https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/2023_12_15_order_no_2023_transition_question_and_answer.pdf
- FERC, **Order on Compliance, Docket No. ER24-2009-000** (4/4/2025, 191 FERC ¶ 61,018): https://ferc.gov/sites/default/files/2025-04/20250404-3081_ER24-2009-000.pdf
- FERC, **Order on Compliance, Docket No. ER24-2009-001** (8/28/2025, 192 FERC ¶ 61,185): https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100026/er24-2009-001.pdf

**Note**: The exact text of the Section 3.4 deposit provisions comes from the most recent (5/3/2025) effective Schedule 22 LGIP and the underlying NEPOOL TC compliance presentations that established the figures; the figures were also reaffirmed in the Order No. 2023 transitional process Q&A documents and the FERC August 28, 2025 order accepting ISO-NE's Second Compliance Filing. If any discrepancy exists between this summary and the official Tariff, the Tariff and applicable Planning/Operating Procedures control.

steps.content: I'll research the ISO New England interconnection requirements and fees for large generators by consulting the relevant sources.
steps.reasoning:
The user is asking about the requirements and fees for submitting a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England. They specifically mention two sources:
1. The ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide
2. ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)

Let me search for these documents to get accurate, up-to-date information.

steps.content: Calling search_web

tool_input.query: ISO-NE Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures LGIP requirements

steps.content: Calling search_web

tool_input.query: ISO New England Interconnection Process Guide large generator application fees

steps.content: I have a good initial overview. Now let me get the specific details on Section 3.4 (Valid Interconnection Request) and the actual application fees from the latest Schedule 22 LGIP document, plus the Appendix 1 form details.
Calling get_page_contents
Calling search_web

      "text": "SEPTEMBER 27, 2023 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, WESTBOROUGH, MA\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER REQUIREMENTS ISO’s Initial Proposed Compliance\n\n### Information Access\n\n##### Order No. 2023 Requirements ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Feasibility Study is eliminated \n• Transmission providers to post and update the heatmap within 30 calendar days after the completion of each cluster study and cluster restudy. \n• Calculated under N-1 conditions and studied based on the power flow model of the transmission system (to approximate (C)NRIS) with specific technical requirements \n• FERC did not adopt the Informational Interconnection Study which was suggested in the NOPR \n• Comply with the heatmap requirement \n• The ISO will focus on compliance with the technically specific requirements for the initial implementation \n• ISO does not currently have significant mapping capability of this nature – will take some time to establish \n• Heatmap is not required until after the transition period – approximately mid 2025 \n\n### Cluster Study Process\n\n| 1. 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### DECEMBER TC MEETING\n\n| Tariff Section | Tariff Change | | | Reason for Change |\n| --- | distinction between |\n| | Affected System. | | | internal and external Affected Systems |\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nCluster Enabling Transmission Upgrade (“CETU”) shall mean new significant transmission line infrastructure that consists of AC transmission lines and related terminal equipment having a nominal voltage rating at or above 115 kV or HVDC transmission lines and HVDC terminal equipment that is identified through the Clustering Enabling Transmission Upgrade Regional Planning Study conducted in accordance with Attachment K, Section 2 of the Tariff as a result of the triggering of conditions in Section 4.2.1.conducted to accommodate the Interconnection Requests for which the conditions identified in Section 4.2.1 have been triggered.\n\nClarified that a Clustering Enabling Transmission Upgrade Regional Planning Study is conducted pursuant to Attachment K\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nCluster Participation DepositCETU Participation Deposit shall mean a Commercial Readiness Deposit as described in the initial and additional deposit due under Sections 4.2.3.2.2 and 4.2.4.4.\n\nClarifies that the CETU Participation Deposit serves as the readiness deposit in the case of a CETU enabled project\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nProportional Impact Method shall mean a technical analysis conducted by the System Operator in accordance with the criteria and parameters specified in the ISO New England Planning Procedures to determine the degree to which each Generating Facility in the Cluster Study contributes to the need for a specific System Network Upgrade.\n\nClarification in response to stakeholder feedback\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nQueue Position shall mean the order of a valid request in the New England Control Area, relative to all other pending requests in the New England Control Area, that is established based upon the date and time of receipt of such request by the System Operator. Requests are comprised of interconnection requests for Generating Facilities, Elective Transmission Upgrades, and requests for transmission service and notification of requests for interconnection to other electric systems, as notified by the other electric systems, that impact the Administered Transmission System.\n\nChanged as a result of the Section 9.3 - Affected System Queue Position being added\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nWithin three (3) Business Days after the close of the Cluster Request Windowits receipt of a valid Interconnection Request, System Operator shall submit a copy of all valid the Interconnection Requests received to Interconnecting Transmission Owner(s).\n\nAdded language back that describes the sharing of requests with Transmission Owners\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nSystem Operator shall study Generating Facilities that include at least one electric storage resource, when studying the charging mode of the electric storage resource(s), using net shoulder system load as defined in ISO New England Planning Procedures. These requests for Interconnection Service also may be subject to other studies at the full Generating Facility CapabilityCapacity to ensure safety and reliability of the system, with the study costs borne by Interconnection Customer.\n\nDeleting language initially copied from the Order. Language is not needed given the ISO’s proposed approach.\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.4.1.1\n\nInterconnection Customer shall submit to System Operator, during a Cluster Request Window, an Interconnection Request in the form of Appendix 1 to this LGIP, a potentially non-refundable initial deposit of $50,000, and a refundable study deposit of $250,000 (for new requests for NR Interconnection Service or CNR Interconnection Service) or $100,000 (for requests to change from existing NR Interconnection Service to CNR Interconnection Service). System Operator shall apply the initial deposit toward the costs incurred by the System Operator associated with the Interconnection Request, the cost of developing the study agreements and their attachments, and the cost of developing the LGIA.       "text": "SEPTEMBER 27, 2023 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, WESTBOROUGH, MA\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER REQUIREMENTS ISO’s Initial Proposed Compliance\n\n### Information Access\n\n##### Order No. 2023 Requirements ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Feasibility Study is eliminated \n• Transmission providers to post and update the heatmap within 30 calendar days after the completion of each cluster study and cluster restudy. \n• Calculated under N-1 conditions and studied based on the power flow model of the transmission system (to approximate (C)NRIS) with specific technical requirements \n• FERC did not adopt the Informational Interconnection Study which was suggested in the NOPR \n• Comply with the heatmap requirement \n• The ISO will focus on compliance with the technically specific requirements for the initial implementation \n• ISO does not currently have significant mapping capability of this nature – will take some time to establish \n• Heatmap is not required until after the transition period – approximately mid 2025 \n\n### Cluster Study Process\n\n| 1. F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the December 2023 TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the December TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### DECEMBER TC MEETING\n\n| Tariff Section | Tariff Change | | | Reason for Change |\n| --- | distinction between |\n| | Affected System. | | | internal and external Affected Systems |\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nCluster Enabling Transmission Upgrade (“CETU”) shall mean new significant transmission line infrastructure that consists of AC transmission lines and related terminal equipment having a nominal voltage rating at or above 115 kV or HVDC transmission lines and HVDC terminal equipment that is identified through the Clustering Enabling Transmission Upgrade Regional Planning Study conducted in accordance with Attachment K, Section 2 of the Tariff as a result of the triggering of conditions in Section 4.2.1.conducted to accommodate the Interconnection Requests for which the conditions identified in Section 4.2.1 have been triggered.\n\nClarified that a Clustering Enabling Transmission Upgrade Regional Planning Study is conducted pursuant to Attachment K\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nCluster Participation DepositCETU Participation Deposit shall mean a Commercial Readiness Deposit as described in the initial and additional deposit due under Sections 4.2.3.2.2 and 4.2.4.4.\n\nClarifies that the CETU Participation Deposit serves as the readiness deposit in the case of a CETU enabled project\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nProportional Impact Method shall mean a technical analysis conducted by the System Operator in accordance with the criteria and parameters specified in the ISO New England Planning Procedures to determine the degree to which each Generating Facility in the Cluster Study contributes to the need for a specific System Network Upgrade.\n\nClarification in response to stakeholder feedback\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nQueue Position shall mean the order of a valid request in the New England Control Area, relative to all other pending requests in the New England Control Area, that is established based upon the date and time of receipt of such request by the System Operator. Requests are comprised of interconnection requests for Generating Facilities, Elective Transmission Upgrades, and requests for transmission service and notification of requests for interconnection to other electric systems, as notified by the other electric systems, that impact the Administered Transmission System.\n\nChanged as a result of the Section 9.3 - Affected System Queue Position being added\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nWithin three (3) Business Days after the close of the Cluster Request Windowits receipt of a valid Interconnection Request, System Operator shall submit a copy of all valid the Interconnection Requests received to Interconnecting Transmission Owner(s).\n\nAdded language back that describes the sharing of requests with Transmission Owners\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nSystem Operator shall study Generating Facilities that include at least one electric storage resource, when studying the charging mode of the electric storage resource(s), using net shoulder system load as defined in ISO New England Planning Procedures. These requests for Interconnection Service also may be subject to other studies at the full Generating Facility CapabilityCapacity to ensure safety and reliability of the system, with the study costs borne by Interconnection Customer.\n\nDeleting language initially copied from the Order. Language is not needed given the ISO’s proposed approach.\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.4.1.1\n\nInterconnection Customer shall submit to System Operator, during a Cluster Request Window, an Interconnection Request in the form of Appendix 1 to this LGIP, a potentially non-refundable initial deposit of $50,000, and a refundable study deposit of $250,000 (for new requests for NR Interconnection Service or CNR Interconnection Service) or $100,000 (for requests to change from existing NR Interconnection Service to CNR Interconnection Service). System Operator shall apply the initial deposit toward the costs incurred by the System Operator associated with the Interconnection Request, the cost of developing the study agreements and their attachments, and the cost of developing the LGIA. The study deposit shall be applied toward the cost of the Cluster Study. For consistency with presented design.\n\nCorrected language to clearly capture proposed deposit design.\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.4.4.1\n\nThe Interconnection Customer shall be responsible for the costs associated with the studies or portions of studies associated with the Internal Affected Systems, including costs associated the requirements of Section I.3.9 of the Tariff.\n\nAdding to clarify that confirmation of no adverse impact on Internal Affected Systems is finalized though the I.3.9 process – and Interconnection Customer is required to pay such costs.\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 4.1.1\n\nSystem Operator shall assign a Queue Position based upon the date and time of receipt of the valid Interconnection Request provided all items required pursuant to the provisions of Section 3.4.2 of this LGIP are received. All Interconnection Requests submitted and validated in a single Cluster Request Window shall be considered equally queued.A higher Queue Position assigned to an Interconnection Request is one that has been placed “earlier” in the queue in relation to another Interconnection Request that is assigned a lower Queue Position. All requests studied in a single Cluster Study shall be considered equally queued. Interconnection Customers that are part of Clusters initiated earlier in time than an instant Cluster Study shall be considered to have a higher Queue Position than Interconnection Customers that are part of Cluster Studies initiated later than an instant Cluster Study.\n\nIncluding pro forma language to clarify queue position.\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 4.2\n\nThe System Operator may use subgroups in the Cluster Study Process. If the System Operator elects n all instances in which System Operator elects to use subgroups in the Cluster Study Process, System Operator must publish the criteria used to define and determine subgroups on its OASIS or public website prior to the opening of a Cluster Request Window.\n\nAs the ISO has discussed in the presentations of Order 2023 compliance, the ISO does not intend to use subgroups in the clustering process. DEFINITIONS \nThe definitions contained in this section are intended to apply in the context of the generator \ninterconnection process provided for in this Schedule 22 (and its appendices). To the extent that the \ndefinitions herein are different than those contained in Section I.2.2 of the Tariff, the definitions provided \nbelow shall control only for purposes of generator interconnections under this Schedule 22. Capitalized \nterms in Schedule 22 that are not defined in this Section I shall have the meanings specified in Section \nI.2.2 of the Tariff. \n\nAdministered Transmission System shall mean the PTF and the Non-PTF.\n\nAdverse System Impact shall mean any significant negative effects on the stability, reliability or operating characteristics of the electric system.\n\nAffected Party shall mean the entity that owns, operates or controls an Affected System, or any other entity operating outside of the New England Control Area that otherwise may be a necessary party to the interconnection process.\n\nAffected System shall mean any electric system that is outside the New England Control Area that may be affected by the proposed interconnection.\n\nAffected System Facilities Construction Agreement shall mean the agreement contained in Appendix 9 to this LGIP that is made between Interconnecting Transmission Owner and Affected System Interconnection Customer to facilitate the construction of and to set forth cost responsibility for necessary Affected System Network Upgrades on the New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Interconnection Customer shall mean any entity that submits an interconnection request for a generating facility to a transmission system outside of the New England Control Area that may cause the need for Affected System Network Upgrades on the New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Network Upgrades shall mean the additions, modifications, and upgrades to New England Transmission System required to accommodate Affected System Interconnection Customer’s proposed interconnection to a transmission system other than New England Transmission System.\n\nAffected System Operator shall mean the entity that operates an Affected System.\n\nAffected System Queue Position shall mean the queue position of an Affected System Interconnection Customer in System Operator’s interconnection queue relative to System Operator’s Interconnection Customers’ Queue Positions.\n\nAffected System Study shall mean the evaluation of Affected System Interconnection Customers’ proposed interconnection(s) to a transmission system outside the New England Control Area that have an impact on the New England Transmission System, as described in Section 9 of this LGIP.\n\nAffected System Study Agreement shall mean the agreement contained in Appendix 7 to this LGIP that is made between System Operator and Affected System Interconnection Customer to conduct an Affected System Study pursuant to Section 9 of this LGIP.\n\nAffected System Study Report shall mean the report issued following completion of an Affected System Study pursuant to Section 9.7 of this LGIP.\n\nAffiliate shall mean, with respect to a corporation, partnership or other entity, each such other corporation, partnership or other entity that directly or indirectly, through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such corporation, partnership or other entity.\n\nApplicable Laws and Regulations shall mean all duly promulgated applicable federal, state and local laws, regulations, rules, ordinances, codes, decrees, judgments, directives, or judicial or administrative orders, permits and other duly authorized actions of any Governmental Authority.\n\nApplicable Reliability Standards shall mean the requirements and guidelines of NERC, the NPCC and the New England Control Area, including publicly available local reliability requirements of Interconnecting Transmission Owners or other Affected Parties or Internal Affected Parties.\n\nAt-Risk Expenditure shall mean money expended for the development of the Generating Facility that cannot be recouped if Interconnection Customer were to withdraw the Interconnection Request for the Generating Facility.       "text": "SEPTEMBER 27, 2023 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, WESTBOROUGH, MA\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER REQUIREMENTS ISO’s Initial Proposed Compliance\n\n### Information Access\n\n##### Order No. 2023 Requirements ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Feasibility Study is eliminated \n• Transmission providers to post and update the heatmap within 30 calendar days after the completion of each cluster study and cluster restudy. \n• Calculated under N-1 conditions and studied based on the power flow model of the transmission system (to approximate (C)NRIS) with specific technical requirements \n• FERC did not adopt the Informational Interconnection Study which was suggested in the NOPR \n• Comply with the heatmap requirement \n• The ISO will focus on compliance with the technically specific requirements for the initial implementation \n• ISO does not currently have significant mapping capability of this nature – will take some time to establish \n• Heatmap is not required until after the transition period – approximately mid 2025 \n\n### Cluster Study Process\n\n| 1. Cluster Request Window (45 Calendar Days (CD)) Interconnection Requests include: application, application fee, study deposit, commercial readiness deposit and demonstration of 90% site control, point of interconnection, CNRIS/NRIS election | | | |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| | | | |\n| 2. Cluster Engagement Window (60 CDs) Single Cluster Study Scoping meeting with all ICs Non-disclosure agreements Cluster Study Agreement | | | |\n| | | | |\n| 3. Cluster Study (270 CDs)* Power flow, short circuit, stability (& EMT analysis) | | | |\n| IRs are equally queued Non-binding cost estimates for network upgrades | | | Cluster Study Cost Allocation Network Upgrade Cost Allocation |\n| | | | |\n| 4. Cluster Restudy (if required) (150 CDs) Additional deposit for 5% of network upgrade cost | | | (see companion presentation on Order No. 2023 Transition for additional details) |\n| assignment from the Cluster Study Power flow, short circuit, stability, EMT analysis Updated network upgrade cost assignment | | | |\n\n*ISO will request a independent entity variation to allow 270 days for the completion of the Cluster Study\n\n### Cluster Study Process, continued\n\nOrder No. 2023 Requirements (see graphic on previous slide) ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Cluster Request Window \n• Cluster Engagement Window \n• Cluster Study \n• Cluster Restudy \n• Adopt these changes with the following exceptions: \n• ISO will request 270 calendar days (instead of the Order’s 150 CDs) to complete the Cluster Study \n• Consistent with established timeline for System Impact Studies in New England \n• Establish a uniform study deposit amount of $250,000*, consistent with existing New England LGIP requirements \n• ISO anticipates opening the first Cluster Request Window in mid-2025 \n• After the completion of the Transitional Cluster Study \n\n*Independent entity variation Order No. 2023 proposes study deposits that vary with Generating Facility size\n\n### Cluster Study Process – ISO-NE Implementation\n\n• Single, region-wide cluster “window” – IRs for all portions of the region would be submitted and studied at the same time \n• However, IRs would be studied separately to the extent possible and would only be studied in gr",

      "text": "192 FERC ¶ 61,185 UNITED STATES OF AMERICA FEDERAL ENERGY REGULATORY COMMISSION\n\nBefore Commissioners: David Rosner, Chairman; Lindsay S. See and Judy W. Chang.\n\nISO New England Inc. Eversource Energy Service Company\n\nDocket No. ER24-2009-001\n\nORDER ON COMPLIANCE AND TARIFF REVISIONS\n\n(Issued August 28, 2025)\n\nOn June 3, 2025, ISO New England Inc. (ISO-NE) and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (PTO AC) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners (PTO) 1 (together, Filing Parties) submitted proposed revisions (Second Compliance Filing) to ISO-NE’s Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (Tariff) 2 in compliance with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A 3 and the Commission’s order on compliance issued on April 4, 2025. 4 As discussed below, we find that Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing complies with the requirements of\n\n1 See infra app. B (listing PTOs).\n\n2 Section II of the Tariff contains the ISO-NE Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT). Schedule 11 of the OATT governs cost allocation for Generating Facility and Elective Transmission Upgrade interconnection-related upgrades. Schedule 22 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (LGIA). Schedule 23 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Small Generator Interconnection Agreement (SGIA). Attachment K to the OATT governs the Regional System Planning Process. Section III of the Tariff contains the Market Rules. Accordingly, we accept the Second Compliance Filing, effective August 12, 2024, as requested. 5\n\n## I. Background\n\nOrder Nos. 2023 and 2023-A require all public utility transmission providers to adopt revised pro forma LGIPs, pro forma LGIAs, pro forma SGIPs, and pro forma SGIAs. 6 These revisions ensure that interconnection customers are able to interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner, and will prevent undue discrimination. 7 In Order No. 2023, the Commission adopted a comprehensive package of reforms in three general categories: (1) reforms to implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process; (2) reforms to increase the speed of interconnection queue processing; and (3) reforms to incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process.\n\nOn May 14, 2024, in Docket No. ER24-2009-000, the Filing Parties 8 in that case submitted proposed revisions to the ISO-NE Tariff to comply with the requirements of Order No. 2023 (First Compliance Filing). 9 In the April Compliance Order, the Commission found that Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing partially complied with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. Order No. 2023, 184 FERC ¶ 61,054 at P 2.\n\n7Id. Specifically, the Commission directed further revisions to ISO-NE’s Tariff to comply with the following requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A: cluster study process, allocation of cluster network upgrade costs, site control, commercial readiness, LGIA deposit, transition process, elimination of reasonable efforts, affected system study process and modeling requirements, affected system pro forma agreements, and modeling and ride-through requirements for non-synchronous generating facilities. 10\n\nII.       "text": "SEPTEMBER 27, 2023 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, WESTBOROUGH, MA\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER REQUIREMENTS ISO’s Initial Proposed Compliance\n\n### Information Access\n\n##### Order No. 2023 Requirements ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Feasibility Study is eliminated \n• Transmission providers to post and update the heatmap within 30 calendar days after the completion of each cluster study and cluster restudy. \n• Calculated under N-1 conditions and studied based on the power flow model of the transmission system (to approximate (C)NRIS) with specific technical requirements \n• FERC did not adopt the Informational Interconnection Study which was suggested in the NOPR \n• Comply with the heatmap requirement \n• The ISO will focus on compliance with the technically specific requirements for the initial implementation \n• ISO does not currently have significant mapping capability of this nature – will take some time to establish \n• Heatmap is not required until after the transition period – approximately mid 2025 \n\n### Cluster Study Process\n\n| 1. RM22-14-___, ER24-2009-___ \nErrata to Revisions to the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets and \nServices Tariff in Compliance with Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A \n\nDear Acting Secretary Reese: On May 14, 2024, pursuant to Rule 1907 of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s (“Commission”) Rules of Practice and Procedure1 and Section 206 of the Federal Power Act (“FPA”),2\n\nISO New England Inc. (“ISO-NE” or “ISO”)3 joined by the New England Power Pool (“NEPOOL”) Participants Committee, and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (“PTO AC”) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners(“PTOs”)(together, the “Filing Parties”) jointly submitted proposed revisions to Sections I, II, and III of the Tariff necessary to comply with the Commission’s landmark order on Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, OrderNos. 2023 and 2023-A. 4 The proposed revisions are collectively referred to as the “Order No. 2023Revisions.”\n\n1 18 C.F.R. § 385.1907. \n2 16 U.S.C. §§ 824e. \n3 Capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined in this filing have the meanings ascribed thereto in \nSection I.2.2 of the ISO-NE Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (the “Tariff”). Section III of the Tariff contains the \nMarket Rules. \n4 Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, Order No. 2023, 184 FERC \n¶ 61,051 (2023), order on reh’g, Order No. 2023-A, 186 FERC ¶ 61,199 (2024). See also, ISO New \nEngland Inc., et al., Revisions to the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff in \nCompliance with Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A; Docket No. ER24-2009-000 (filed May 14, 2024) (“May \n14 Filing”). \n\nIt has come to the attention of the ISO that the May 14 Filing contained a minor error that warrants correction. Specifically, the last full paragraph on page 67 of the transmittal letter inadvertently omitted language that should have been included as part of explanation of the Filing Parties’ proposed requirements related to operating assumptions for storage facilities, and absent that language the proposal could be misunderstood. The paragraph should have read and is being revised as follows: “Rather than incorporate specific operating restrictions in each storage facility’s Interconnection Agreement, the Filing Parties propose to rely on the ISO’s SCED process to prevent storage devices from being dispatched to charge at load levels higher than the peak shoulder load under which the facility was studied, if such charging would cause a system overload. However, the SCED would allow the battery to charge at higher than shoulder loads, as long as no violation would be caused in operations by the charging.” To avoid any potential confusion, attached hereto is a copy of the transmittal letter updated to reflect these changes. The Filing Parties regret any inconvenience that may result from this minor omission, and respectfully request that the Commission accept the Order No. 2023 Revisions with an effective date of August 12, 2024, and issue an order on or before that date. Schedule 23 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Small Generator Interconnection Agreement (SGIA). Attachment K to the OATT governs the Regional System Planning Process. Section III of the Tariff contains the Market Rules. Accordingly, we accept the Second Compliance Filing, effective August 12, 2024, as requested. 5\n\n## I. Background\n\nOrder Nos. 2023 and 2023-A require all public utility transmission providers to adopt revised pro forma LGIPs, pro forma LGIAs, pro forma SGIPs, and pro forma SGIAs. 6 These revisions ensure that interconnection customers are able to interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner, and will prevent undue discrimination. 7 In Order No. 2023, the Commission adopted a comprehensive package of reforms in three general categories: (1) reforms to implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process; (2) reforms to increase the speed of interconnection queue processing; and (3) reforms to incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process.\n\nOn May 14, 2024, in Docket No. ER24-2009-000, the Filing Parties 8 in that case submitted proposed revisions to the ISO-NE Tariff to comply with the requirements of Order No. 2023 (First Compliance Filing). 9 In the April Compliance Order, the Commission found that Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing partially complied with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. Accordingly, the Commission\n\n5 See infra app. A (providing eTariff records).\n\n6 The pro forma LGIP and pro forma LGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities larger than 20 MW. The pro forma SGIP and pro forma SGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities no larger than 20 MW. Order No. 2023, 184 FERC ¶ 61,054 at P 2.\n\n7Id. P 1.\n\n8 The filing parties that submitted the First Compliance Filing in Docket No. ER24-2009-000 also included the New England Power Pool (NEPOOL). The April Compliance Order addressed and accepted the proposed revisions submitted in Docket No. ER24-2007-000.\n\nDocket No. ER24-2009-001 - 3 accepted Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing in part, effective August 12, 2024, and directed the Filing Parties to submit a further compliance filing within 60 days of the issuance of the April Compliance Order. Specifically, the Commission directed further revisions to ISO-NE’s Tariff to comply with the following requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A: cluster study process, allocation of cluster network upgrade costs, site control, commercial readiness, LGIA deposit, transition process, elimination of reasonable efforts, affected system study process and modeling requirements, affected system pro forma agreements, and modeling and ride-through requirements for non-synchronous generating facilities. 10\n\nII. Second Compliance Filing\n\nFiling Parties state that through the Second Compliance Filing they propose revisions to ISO-NE Tariff section I.2.2, Tariff Schedules 11, 22 (ISO-NE LGIP), 23 (ISO-NE SGIP), and Tariff section III.13 in compliance with the April Compliance Order. Filing Parties also state that they have made conforming changes to the ISO-NE SGIP and Tariff Schedule 25 (Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures) 11 consistent with the directives of the April Compliance Order.\n\n10 April Compliance Order, 191 FERC ¶ 61,018 at PP 77, 85, 100, 102, 133, 135, 152-153, 158, 186, 197, 218, 227, 280-283.\n\n11 Schedule 25 contains interconnection requirements for Elective Transmission Upgrades. The Tariff defines an “Elective Transmission Upgrade” as follows:\n\nElective Transmission Upgrade . . . shall mean a new Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is interconnecting to the Administered Transmission System, or an upgrade to an existing Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is part of or interconnected to the Administered Transmission System for which the Interconnection Customer has agreed to pay all of the costs of said Elective Transmission Upgrade and of any additions or modifications to the Administered Transmission System that are required to accommodate the Elective Transmission Upgrade. An Elective Transmission Upgrade is not a Generator Interconnection Related Upgrade, a Regional Transmission Upgrade, or a Market Efficiency Transmission Upgrade.\n\nTariff, § II, Schedule 25 (12.0.0), § 1 (Definitions), Elective Transmission Upgrade.\n\nFiling Parties request that the proposed Tariff revisions become effective on August 12, 2024. 12\n\nIII. Notice of Filing and Responsive Pleadings\n\nNotice of Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing was published in the Federal Register, 90 Fed. Reg. 24275 (June 9, 2025), with comments, interventions, and protests due on or before June 24, 2025. NEPOOL filed supportive comments requesting that the Commission accept the Second Compliance Filing without delay, condition, or modification. 13\n\n## IV. Discussion\n\nAs discussed below, we find that Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing complies with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. All other deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be paid in cash and delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer within the period specified in the respective provision. A deposit will not be considered received until it is in the System Operator’s bank account or, in the case of a Letter of Credit provided as a Commercial Readiness Deposit, the Letter of Credit is accepted by System Operator. Deposits that must be submitted to the Interconnecting Transmission Owner may be submitted in a form acceptable to the Interconnecting Transmission Owner.\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\nStill require cash for study deposits\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit, cont.\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP\n\n3.4.2\n\n(vi) a Commercial Readiness Deposit equal to two times the study deposit described in Section 3.1.1.1 of this LGIP in the form of an irrevocable letter of credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or cash where cash deposits shall be treated according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP. The letter of credit must specify the name of the Interconnection Customer to which the Commercial Readiness Deposit corresponds. This Commercial Readiness Deposit is refunded to Interconnection Customer according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP,\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit, cont.\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP 5.1.1.1 & 5.1.1.2\n\n…in the form of an irrevocable letter of credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or cash where cash deposits shall be treated according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP. The letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which the Commercial Readiness Deposit corresponds.\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\n### TRANSITION RELATED UPDATES\n\n### Transition Process Dates and Milestones*\n\n• “Effective Date” – ISO is proposing an Effective Date of May 31, 2024 \n• Execution of Transitional Serial Interconnection Facilities or Transitional Cluster Study Agreement is due no later than 60 Calendar Days (CD) from the Effective Date and study would commence thereafter \n• “Eligibility Date” – Consistent with Order 2023, an April 1 compliance filing date results in an Eligibility Date of May 1, 2024. This means that Interconnection Customers with a valid IR as of May 1, 2024 will be eligible to elect to proceed with a transition study or elect to withdraw from the interconnection queue without penalties. – On May 31, the ISO will issue a Transitional Serial Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement or a Transitional Cluster Study Agreement to Interconnection Customers with eligible IRs \n• Interconnection Requests that are not valid and have not been assigned Queue Position as of the Eligibility Date will be withdrawn by the ISO without further opportunity to cure any deficiencies (withdrawal penalties will not apply) – The ISO will not accept any Interconnection Requests submitted after the Eligibility Date until the first Cluster Entry Window Opens in 2025 – Ongoing study work for IRs for which an SIS is underway as of the Eligibility Date will continue through the Effective Date \n• Results of those studies will be provided for information purposes only and will not effect a project’s eligibility status with respect to the Transitional Cluster Study \n\n*All dates assume FERC acceptance of ISO-NE’s compliance proposal\n\n### Allowing for Requests to Pause On-going Studies Before Transition\n\n• Stakeholders at the TC and offline have requested that the ISO consider a process whereby ICs can request that the ISO cease the study process for projects that are actively being studied or about to begin if they have not yet started \n• The ISO has reviewed this request and identified the following applicable language in Section 2.5 of the LGIP – Parties that must perform a specific obligation under a provision of the Standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedure or Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement within a specified time period shall use Reasonable Efforts to complete such obligation within the applicable time period. A Party may, in the exercise of reasonable discretion and within the time period set forth by the applicable procedure or agreement, request that the relevant Party consent to a mutually agreeable alternative time schedule, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld. \n\n### Pausing On-going Studies Before Transition\n\n• Existing functionality in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) allows the IC to see: – the expected completion date of an on-going study – when a study is expected to start – if it has not yet started \n• The ISO is proposing to allow an IC to voluntarily request that the ISO cease study work, i.e. “consent to a mutually agreeable alternative time schedule” \n• The option would only be provided to those IRs where the ISO has determined that the interconnection study will not be completed by the May 1, 2024 Eligibility Date under Order 2023 – Where the ISO is forecasting that the study will be completed by the eligibility deadline, the ISO will continue the study work – The ISO will also continue study work for those IRs that decline the option to have the study work paused \n\n### Pausing On-going Studies Before Transition Next Steps\n\n• ISO requests feedback from the TC on this proposal to IRTT Customer Support \n• ISO will complete review of this proposal \n• A final decision would take place in early January and, if we decide to proceed, ISO would communicate with the relevant ICs during the week of January 8 \n\n### Transition Process – Schedule of Deposits\n\n1.       "text": "Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n### In this section\n\n- Key Projects\n- Asset Condition Reviewer Key Project\n- Capacity Auction Reforms Key Project\n- Operational Impacts of Extreme Weather Events Key Project\n- Order No. 1920 Key Project\n- Order No. 2222 Key Project\n- Order No. 2023 Key Project\n- Implemented Key Projects\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Annual Work Plan\n- Participant Readiness Project Outlook\n- Training\n- Wholesale Markets Project Plan\n\nAccess documents and resources related to Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements Key Project.\n\n### Project Overview\n\nOn July 28, 2023, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) issued Order No. 2023 (RM22-14-000) mandating significant reforms to generator interconnection procedures and agreements to ensure that interconnection customers can interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, timely, and fair manner. The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006, and 845, and are intended to address transmission interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies as those resources proliferate. On March 21, 2024, FERC issued Order No. 2023-A with some incremental reforms to the Order.\n\nComplying with the Order requires significant changes to the manner in which the ISO administers the transmission interconnection processes and how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes.\n\nPrimary elements of the Order include:\n\n- Implementing a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, eliminating New England’s current serial first-come, first-served study process\n- Speeding up interconnection queue processing through improved processes, deadlines, and penalties\n- Incorporating technological advancements into the interconnection process, including modeling and performance standards for inverter-based resources\n\n### Learning Resources\n\nBelow are important Q&A and training materials for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- Read questions and answers on the Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition\n- Explore Order No. 2023 and Transitional Cluster Study training resources\n- Access Knowledge Articles in Ask ISO on the 2025 Transitional Interconnection Process\n- View the ISO Public Interconnection Queue\n- Read the Interconnection Process Guide on the ISO website\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) Requirements\n\nBelow are the CRD instructions and other forms required for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- CRD Instructions, FAQs, and ISO-NE Approved Templates\n- Surety Bond Form\n- Letter of Credit (LC) Form\n- List of Approved Surety Issuers\n\nThe Order No. 2023 project is just one of several key projects at the ISO, which continuously undertakes a variety of short- and long-term projects to enhance the region’s competitive wholesale electricity markets and ensure reliable operation of the power grid.\n\n## Project-Related
