      "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study. Notwithstanding any other provision of the Tariff, an Interconnection Customer may seek to participate in both the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff and the Transitional Cluster Study simultaneously. If the Interconnection Customer achieves CNR Interconnection Service through Section III.13.1.1.2A, it may withdraw from the Transitional Cluster Study without penalty and be refunded any remaining study deposits associated with the Transitional Cluster Study. If the Interconnection Customer does not enter, or complete, the process described in either Section III.13.1.1.2A or the Transitional Cluster Study, the System Operator shall reduce the Interconnection Customer’s Interconnection Request to NR Interconnection Service.\n\nIn response to Stakeholder suggestion, captures opportunities to establish CNRIS through the Transitional CNR Group Study or Transitional Cluster\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nIn accordance with Section III.13.1.1.2.3A, System Operator shall conduct a Transitional CNR Group Study following the effective date of this LGIP. An Interconnection Customer with an assigned Queue Position as of thirty (30) Calendar Days after April 1, 2024 may participate in the Transitional CNR Group Study, and consistent with Section II.48 of the Tariff, achieve CNR Interconnection Service. Any Interconnection Customer seeking to establish CNR Interconnection Service through this study must (1) have a valid Interconnection Request seeking CNR Interconnection Service, (2) submit a New Capacity Show of Interest Form to participate in the interim reconfiguration auction qualification process, (3) have not secured a Capacity Supply Obligation in the eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or earlier, (4) have a completed System Impact Study or Interconnection Agreement establishing NR Interconnection Service, and 5) have a Commercial Operation Date prior to June 1, 2028.\n\nEligibility for Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nSystem Operator shall conduct the study by performing an overlapping impacts analysis in the manner used for CNR Group Studies conducted prior to the effective date of this LGIP and as described in ISO Section III.13.1.1.2.3A and the ISO New England Planning Procedures. The Transitional CNR Group Study shall assure that the Interconnection Customer’s Large Generating Facility can be interconnected in a manner that ensures intra-zonal deliverability by avoidance of the redispatch of other Capacity Network Resources and Elective Transmission Upgrades with CNI Interconnection Service, in accordance with the CC Interconnection Standard and as detailed in the ISO New England Planning Procedures.\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection Requests for CNR Interconnection Service and CNI Interconnection Service submitted to the System Operator pursuant to Schedules 22, 23, or 25 of Section II of the Tariff and interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates pursuant to the applicable state tariff, rules or procedures that have obtained Proposed Plan Application approval from the System Operator pursuant to Section I.3.9 of the Tariff shall be included in the Transitional CNR Group Study in order of submission/approval (the dates of submission shall be used for Interconnection Requests submitted to the System Operator and the dates of Proposed Plan Application approval shall be used for interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates).\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study, continued\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company a",

      "text": "May 31, 2024\n\nVIA ELECTRONIC FILING \nThe Honorable Debbie-Anne Reese, Acting Secretary \nFederal Energy Regulatory Commission \n888 First Street, NE \nWashington, DC 20426 \nRe: ISO New England Inc.; Docket Nos. Transmission, Markets and \nServices Tariff in Compliance with Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A \n\nDear Acting Secretary Reese: On May 14, 2024, pursuant to Rule 1907 of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s (“Commission”) Rules of Practice and Procedure1 and Section 206 of the Federal Power Act (“FPA”),2\n\nISO New England Inc. (“ISO-NE” or “ISO”)3 joined by the New England Power Pool (“NEPOOL”) Participants Committee, and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (“PTO AC”) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners(“PTOs”)(together, the “Filing Parties”) jointly submitted proposed revisions to Sections I, II, and III of the Tariff necessary to comply with the Commission’s landmark order on Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, OrderNos. 2023 and 2023-A. 4 The proposed revisions are collectively referred to as the “Order No. 2023Revisions.”\n\n1 18 C.F.R. § 385.1907. \n2 16 U.S.C. §§ 824e. \n3 Capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined in this filing have the meanings ascribed thereto in \nSection I.2.2 of the ISO-NE Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (the “Tariff”). Section II of the \nTariff contains the Open Access Transmission Tariff (the “OATT”). Section III of the Tariff contains the \nMarket Rules. \n4 Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, Order No. 2023, 184 FERC \n¶ 61,051 (2023), order on reh’g, Order No. 2023-A, 186 FERC ¶ 61,199 (2024). See also, ISO New \nEngland Inc., et al., Revisions to the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff in \nCompliance with Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A; Docket No. ER24-2009-000 (filed May 14, 2024) (“May \n14 Filing”). \n\nIt has come to the attention of the ISO that the May 14 Filing contained a minor error that warrants correction. Specifically, the last full paragraph on page 67 of the transmittal letter inadvertently omitted language that should have been included as part of explanation of the Filing Parties’ proposed requirements related to operating assumptions for storage facilities, and absent that language the proposal could be misunderstood. The paragraph should have read and is being revised as follows: “Rather than incorporate specific operating restrictions in each storage facility’s Interconnection Agreement, the Filing Parties propose to rely on the ISO’s SCED process to prevent storage devices from being dispatched to charge at load levels higher than the peak shoulder load under which the facility was studied, if such charging would cause a system overload. However, the SCED would allow the battery to charge at higher than shoulder loads, as long as no violation would be caused in operations by the charging.” To avoid any potential confusion, attached hereto is a copy of the transmittal letter updated to reflect these changes. The Filing Parties regret any inconvenience that may result from this minor omission, and respectfully request that the Commission accept the Order No. 2023 Revisions with an effective date of August 12, 2024, and issue an order on or before that date. Should you have any questions regarding this errata filing, please do not hesitate to contact the undersigned.\n\nRespectfully submitted, ISO NEW ENGLAND INC. By: /s/ Graham Jesmer ISO New England Inc. One Sullivan Road Holyoke, MA 01040-2841 (413) 540-4557 gjesmer@iso-ne.com Counsel for ISO New England\n\nNEPOOL PARTICIPANTS \nCOMMITTEE \nBy: /s/ Eric K. Runge \nEric K. Runge \nDay Pitney LLP \nOne Federal Street \nBoston, MA 02110 \n(617) 345-4735 \nekrunge@daypitney.com \nCounsel for New England Power \nPool Participants Committee \nPARTICIPATING TRANSMISSION \nOWNERS ADMINISTRATIVE \nCOMMITTEE \nBy: /s/ Mary E. RM22-14-___, ER24-___-000\n\nREQUEST FOR AN EFFECTIVE DATE OF AUGUST 12, 2024, AND AN ORDER ON OR BEFORE THAT DATE\n\nDear Acting Secretary Reese:\n\nPursuant to Rule 1907 of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s (“Commission”) Rules of Practice and Procedure 1 and Section 206 of the Federal Power Act (“FPA”), 2 ISO New England Inc. (“ISO-NE” or “ISO”) 3joined by the New England Power Pool (“NEPOOL”) Participants Committee, and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (“PTO AC”) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners (“PTOs”) (together, the “Filing Parties”) 4 hereby jointly submit this transmittal letter and proposed revisions to Sections I, II, and III of the Tariff that are\n\n1 18 C.F.R. § 385.1907. \n2 16 U.S.C. §§ 824e. \n3 Capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined in this filing have the meanings ascribed thereto in \nSection I.2.2 of the ISO-NE Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (the “Tariff”). Section II of the \nTariff contains the Open Access Transmission Tariff (the “OATT”). Section III of the Tariff contains the \nMarket Rules. \n4 The Filing Parties note that the rights under Section 205 of the FPA to modify terms, conditions and rates \nin the Tariff that are being filed herein are held and exercised solely by the ISO, with the limited exception \nof Schedule 11 of the OATT over which the PTOs jointly hold the Section 205 rights, and Schedules 22 \nand 23 of the OATT over which the ISO shares Section 205 rights with the PTOs in the manner specified \nin Article 3.04 of the Transmission Operating Agreement between the PTOs and the ISO (the “TOA”). \nNEPOOL, which pursuant to the Participants Agreement provides the sole Participant Process for advisory \nvoting on ISO matters, supported the changes reflected in this filing and, accordingly, joins in this filing. \nThe Honorable Debbie-Anne Reese, Acting Secretary \nMay 14, 2024 \nPage 2 of 80 \n\nnecessary to comply with the Commission’s landmark order on Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements, Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. 5 The proposed revisions are collectively referred to as the “Order No. 2023 Revisions.”\n\nThe Order No. 2023 Revisions being submitted in this filing, together with the changes included in the companion filing being submitted concurrently pursuant to Section 205 of the FPA (referred to as the “Order No. 2023 Related Changes”), 6are necessary to achieve compliance with Order No. 2023, in particular, the requirement to move to a first-ready, first-served cluster study construct in which all Interconnection Requests included in a given cluster are considered equally queued, in a seamless and implementable manner. (ISO-NE) and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (PTO AC) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners (PTO) 1 (together, Filing Parties) submitted proposed revisions (Second Compliance Filing) to ISO-NE’s Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (Tariff) 2 in compliance with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A 3 and the Commission’s order on compliance issued on April 4, 2025. 4 As discussed below, we find that Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing complies with the requirements of\n\n1 See infra app. B (listing PTOs).\n\n2 Section II of the Tariff contains the ISO-NE Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT). Schedule 22 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (LGIA). Schedule 23 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Small Generator Interconnection Agreement (SGIA). Attachment K to the OATT governs the Regional System Planning Process. Section III of the Tariff contains the Market Rules. Accordingly, we accept the Second Compliance Filing, effective August 12, 2024, as requested. 5\n\n## I. Background\n\nOrder Nos. 2023 and 2023-A require all public utility transmission providers to adopt revised pro forma LGIPs, pro forma LGIAs, pro forma SGIPs, and pro forma SGIAs. 6 These revisions ensure that interconnection customers are able to interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner, and will prevent undue discrimination. 7 In Order No. 2023, the Commission adopted a comprehensive package of reforms in three general categories: (1) reforms to implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process; (2) reforms to increase the speed of interconnection queue processing; and (3) reforms to incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process.\n\nOn May 14, 2024, in Docket No. ER24-2009-000, the Filing Parties 8 in that case submitted proposed revisions to the ISO-NE Tariff to comply with the requirements of Order No. 2023 (First Compliance Filing). 9 In the April Compliance Order, the Commission found that Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing partially complied with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. Accordingly, the Commission\n\n5 See infra app. A (providing eTariff records).\n\n6 The pro forma LGIP and pro forma LGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities larger than 20 MW. The pro forma SGIP and pro forma SGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities no larger than 20 MW. Order No. 2023, 184 FERC ¶ 61,054 at P 2.\n\n7Id. P 1.\n\n8 The filing parties that submitted the First Compliance Filing in Docket No. ER24-2009-000 also included the New England Power Pool (NEPOOL). Noting this difference, for simplicity this order refers to the filing parties in either proceeding as “Filing Parties.”\n\n9 Filing Parties also submitted a companion filing consisting of revisions not directly required by Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A in Docket No. ER24-2007-000. The April Compliance Order addressed and accepted the proposed revisions submitted in Docket No. ER24-2007-000.\n\nDocket No. ER24-2009-001 - 3 accepted Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing in part, effective August 12, 2024, and directed the Filing Parties to submit a further compliance filing within 60 days of the issuance of the April Compliance Order. Specifically, the Commission directed further revisions to ISO-NE’s Tariff to comply with the following requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A: cluster study process, allocation of cluster network upgrade costs, site control, commercial readiness, LGIA deposit, transition process, elimination of reasonable efforts, affected system study process and modeling requirements, affected system pro forma agreements, and modeling and ride-through requirements for non-synchronous generating facilities. 10\n\nII. Second Compliance Filing\n\nFiling Parties state that through the Second Compliance Filing they propose revisions to ISO-NE Tariff section I.2.2, Tariff Schedules 11, 22 (ISO-NE LGIP), 23 (ISO-NE SGIP), and Tariff section III.13 in compliance with the April Compliance Order. Filing Parties also state that they have made conforming changes to the ISO-NE SGIP and Tariff Schedule 25 (Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures) 11 consistent with the directives of the April Compliance Order.\n\n10 April Compliance Order, 191 FERC ¶ 61,018 at PP 77, 85, 100, 102, 133, 135, 152-153, 158, 186, 197, 218, 227, 280-283.\n\n11 Schedule 25 contains interconnection requirements for Elective Transmission Upgrades. The Tariff defines an “Elective Transmission Upgrade” as follows:\n\nElective Transmission Upgrade . . . shall mean a new Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is interconnecting to the Administered Transmission System, or an upgrade to an existing Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is part of or interconnected to the Administered Transmission System for which the Interconnection Customer has agreed to pay all of the costs of said Elective Transmission Upgrade and of any additions or modifications to the Administered Transmission System that are required to accommodate the Elective Transmission Upgrade. An Elective Transmission Upgrade is not a Generator Interconnection Related Upgrade, a Regional Transmission Upgrade, or a Market Efficiency Transmission Upgrade.\n\nTariff, § II, Schedule 25 (12.0.0), § 1 (Definitions), Elective Transmission Upgrade.\n\nFiling Parties request that the proposed Tariff revisions become effective on August 12, 2024. 12\n\nIII. Notice of Filing and Responsive Pleadings\n\nNotice of Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing was published in the Federal Register, 90 Fed. Reg. 24275 (June 9, 2025), with comments, interventions, and protests due on or before June 24, 2025.       "text": "D E C E M B E R 2 1 , 2 0 2 3 | N E P O O L T R A N S M I S S I O N C O M M I T T E E | D O U B L E T R E E H O T E L , W E S T B O R O U G H , M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n##### Summary of Tariff Redline Design Details\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted the initial draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the key components of the compliance design and responds to several Stakeholder comments and questions \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP \n• The pre-application process has been maintained in the SGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\n### ACCEPTABLE FORM OF READINESS DEPOSITS\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer or through the provision and maintenance of a Letter of Credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website. All other deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be paid in cash and delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer within the period specified in the respective provision. A deposit will not be considered received until it is in the System Operator’s bank account or, in the case of a Letter of Credit provided as a Commercial Readiness Deposit, the Letter of Credit is accepted by System Operator. Deposits that must be submitted to the Interconnecting Transmission Owner may be submitted in a form acceptable to the Interconnecting Transmission Owner.\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\nStill require cash for study deposits\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit, cont.\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP\n\n3.4.2\n\n(vi) a Commercial Readiness Deposit equal to two times the study deposit described in Section 3.1.1.1 of this LGIP in the form of an irrevocable letter of credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or cash where cash deposits shall be treated according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP. The letter of credit must specify the name of the Interconnection Customer to which the Commercial Readiness Deposit corresponds. This Commercial Readiness Deposit is refunded to Interconnection Customer according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP,\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit, cont.\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP 5.1.1.1 & 5.1.1.2\n\n…in the form of an irrevocable letter of credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or cash where cash deposits shall be treated according to Section 3.7 of this LGIP. The letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which the Commercial Readiness Deposit corresponds.\n\nIncorporating Order No. 2023 proposal to allow Letter of Credit for Commercial Readiness Deposits\n\n### TRANSITION RELATED UPDATES\n\n### Transition Process Dates and Milestones*\n\n• “Effective Date” – ISO is proposing an Effective Date of May 31, 2024 \n• Execution of Transitional Serial Interconnection Facilities or Transitional Cluster Study Agreement is due no later than 60 Calendar Days (CD) from the Effective Date and study would commence thereafter \n• “Eligibility Date” – Consistent with Order 2023, an April 1 compliance filing date results in an Eligibility Date of May 1, 2024. This means that Interconnection Customers with a valid IR as of May 1, 2024 will be eligible to elect to proceed with a transition study or elect to withdraw from the interconnection queue without penalties. – On May 31, the ISO will issue a Transitional Serial Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement or a Transitional Cluster Study Agreement to Interconnection Customers with eligible IRs \n• Interconnection Requests that are not valid and have not been assigned Queue Position as of the Eligibility Date will be withdrawn by the ISO without further opportunity to cure any deficiencies (withdrawal penalties will not apply) – The ISO will not accept any Interconnection Requests submitted after the Eligibility Date until the first Cluster Entry Window Opens in 2025 – Ongoing study work for IRs for which an SIS is underway as of the Eligibility Date will continue through the Effective Date \n• Results of those studies will be provided for information purposes only and will not effect a project’s eligibility status with respect to the Transitional Cluster Study \n\n*All dates assume FERC acceptance of ISO-NE’s compliance proposal\n\n### Allowing for Requests to Pause On-going Studies Before Transition\n\n• Stakeholders at the TC and offline have requested that the ISO consider a process whereby ICs can request that the ISO cease the study process for projects that are actively being studied or about to begin if they have not yet started \n• The ISO has reviewed this request and identified the following applicable language in Section 2.5 of the LGIP – Parties that must perform a specific obligation under a provision of the Standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedure or Standard Large Generator Interconnection Agreement within a specified time period shall use Reasonable Efforts to complete such obligation within the applicable time period. A Party may, in the exercise of reasonable discretion and within the time period set forth by the applicable procedure or agreement, request that the relevant Party consent to a mutually agreeable alternative time schedule, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld. \n\n### Pausing On-going Studies Before Transition\n\n• Existing functionality in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) allows the IC to see: – the expected completion date of an on-going study – when a study is expected to start – if it has not yet started \n• The ISO is proposing to allow an IC to voluntarily request that the ISO cease study work, i.e. “consent to a mutually agreeable alternative time schedule” \n• The option would only be provided to those IRs where the ISO has determined that the interconnection study will not be completed by the May 1, 2024 Eligibility Date under Order 2023 – Where the ISO is forecasting that the study will be completed by the eligibility deadline, the ISO will continue the study work – The ISO will also continue study work for those IRs that decline the option to have the study work paused \n\n### Pausing On-going Studies Before Transition Next Steps\n\n• ISO requests feedback from the TC on this proposal to IRTT Customer Support \n• ISO will complete review of this proposal \n• A final decision would take place in early January and, if we decide to proceed, ISO would communicate with the relevant ICs during the week of January 8 \n\n### Transition Process – Schedule of Deposits\n\n1.       "text": "Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Order No. 2023 Key Project\n\n### In this section\n\n- Key Projects\n- Asset Condition Reviewer Key Project\n- Capacity Auction Reforms Key Project\n- Operational Impacts of Extreme Weather Events Key Project\n- Order No. 1920 Key Project\n- Order No. 2222 Key Project\n- Order No. 2023 Key Project\n- Implemented Key Projects\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Annual Work Plan\n- Participant Readiness Project Outlook\n- Training\n- Wholesale Markets Project Plan\n\nAccess documents and resources related to Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements Key Project.\n\n### Project Overview\n\nOn July 28, 2023, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) issued Order No. 2023 (RM22-14-000) mandating significant reforms to generator interconnection procedures and agreements to ensure that interconnection customers can interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, timely, and fair manner. The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006, and 845, and are intended to address transmission interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies as those resources proliferate. On March 21, 2024, FERC issued Order No. 2023-A with some incremental reforms to the Order.\n\nComplying with the Order requires significant changes to the manner in which the ISO administers the transmission interconnection processes and how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes.\n\nPrimary elements of the Order include:\n\n- Implementing a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, eliminating New England’s current serial first-come, first-served study process\n- Speeding up interconnection queue processing through improved processes, deadlines, and penalties\n- Incorporating technological advancements into the interconnection process, including modeling and performance standards for inverter-based resources\n\n### Learning Resources\n\nBelow are important Q&A and training materials for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- Read questions and answers on the Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition\n- Explore Order No. 2023 and Transitional Cluster Study training resources\n- Access Knowledge Articles in Ask ISO on the 2025 Transitional Interconnection Process\n- View the ISO Public Interconnection Queue\n- Read the Interconnection Process Guide on the ISO website\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD) Requirements\n\nBelow are the CRD instructions and other forms required for participation in the interconnection process for the upcoming Order No. 2023 Transitional Cluster Study:\n\n- CRD Instructions, FAQs, and ISO-NE Approved       "text": "FEBRUARY 15, 2024 | NEPOOL TRANSMISSION COMMITTEE | DOUBLETREE HOTEL, MILFORD, M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n#### Summary of Tariff Redline Updates since the January TC Meeting\n\n## Order No. 2023 – Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\nISO-NE PUBLIC 2\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted an updated draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the major updates to the Tariff redlines since the January TC meeting \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\nISO-NE PUBLIC ISO-NE INTERNAL USE\n\n### UPDATES TO THE TARIFF REDLINES SINCE THE\n\n### JANUARY TC MEETING\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be (a) delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer, or (b) through the provision and maintenance of an irrevocable lLetter of cCredit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator and included on the List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers, as described on the System Operator’s public website, or (c) a combination thereof. Each letter of credit must specify the Interconnection Request to which it corresponds. All costs associated with obtaining a letter of credit shall be borne by the Interconnection Customer. In the event that System Operator identifies an administrative deficiency with a submitted letter of credit, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days to cure the deficiency..\n\nIn response to stakeholder suggestion, a cure period has been included for certain Letter of Credit (LOC) issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.1\n\nIf the System Operator removes the financial institution from the list, Interconnection Customer shall have five (5) Business Days from the date on which System Operator provides notice of such removal to replace the letter of credit with a letter of credit from a financial institution on the list. The System Operator may extend this cure period in its sole discretion. Failure to cure a deficiency within the periods prescribed in this Section 3.1 shall result in the withdrawal of the Interconnection Request pursuant to Section 3.7 of the LGIP without further opportunity to cure.\n\nAdded cure period for certain LOC issues. Note that an LOC, in the correct amount, must have been submitted by the relevant deadline\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Definitions\n\nTransitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study (“Transitional CNR Group Study”) shall mean the study performed by the System Operator under Section III.13.1.1.2.3A of the Tariff and Section 5.1.1.3 of this LGIP.\n\nDefinition for the Transitional Capacity Network Resource Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 3.2.1.3\n\nAny Interconnection Customer with a valid Interconnection Request for CNR Interconnection Service that has a completed Interconnection System Impact Study as of May 1, 2024, but that has not received a Capacity Supply Obligation through eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or an earlier auction may: 1) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff or 2) seek to complete the process for obtaining CNR Interconnection Service through the Transitional Cluster Study. Notwithstanding any other provision of the Tariff, an Interconnection Customer may seek to participate in both the process described in Section III.13.1.1.2A of the Tariff and the Transitional Cluster Study simultaneously. If the Interconnection Customer achieves CNR Interconnection Service through Section III.13.1.1.2A, it may withdraw from the Transitional Cluster Study without penalty and be refunded any remaining study deposits associated with the Transitional Cluster Study. If the Interconnection Customer does not enter, or complete, the process described in either Section III.13.1.1.2A or the Transitional Cluster Study, the System Operator shall reduce the Interconnection Customer’s Interconnection Request to NR Interconnection Service.\n\nIn response to Stakeholder suggestion, captures opportunities to establish CNRIS through the Transitional CNR Group Study or Transitional Cluster\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nIn accordance with Section III.13.1.1.2.3A, System Operator shall conduct a Transitional CNR Group Study following the effective date of this LGIP. An Interconnection Customer with an assigned Queue Position as of thirty (30) Calendar Days after April 1, 2024 may participate in the Transitional CNR Group Study, and consistent with Section II.48 of the Tariff, achieve CNR Interconnection Service. Any Interconnection Customer seeking to establish CNR Interconnection Service through this study must (1) have a valid Interconnection Request seeking CNR Interconnection Service, (2) submit a New Capacity Show of Interest Form to participate in the interim reconfiguration auction qualification process, (3) have not secured a Capacity Supply Obligation in the eighteenth Forward Capacity Auction or earlier, (4) have a completed System Impact Study or Interconnection Agreement establishing NR Interconnection Service, and 5) have a Commercial Operation Date prior to June 1, 2028.\n\nEligibility for Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nSystem Operator shall conduct the study by performing an overlapping impacts analysis in the manner used for CNR Group Studies conducted prior to the effective date of this LGIP and as described in ISO Section III.13.1.1.2.3A and the ISO New England Planning Procedures. The Transitional CNR Group Study shall assure that the Interconnection Customer’s Large Generating Facility can be interconnected in a manner that ensures intra-zonal deliverability by avoidance of the redispatch of other Capacity Network Resources and Elective Transmission Upgrades with CNI Interconnection Service, in accordance with the CC Interconnection Standard and as detailed in the ISO New England Planning Procedures.\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection Requests for CNR Interconnection Service and CNI Interconnection Service submitted to the System Operator pursuant to Schedules 22, 23, or 25 of Section II of the Tariff and interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates pursuant to the applicable state tariff, rules or procedures that have obtained Proposed Plan Application approval from the System Operator pursuant to Section I.3.9 of the Tariff shall be included in the Transitional CNR Group Study in order of submission/approval (the dates of submission shall be used for Interconnection Requests submitted to the System Operator and the dates of Proposed Plan Application approval shall be used for interconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company affiliates).\n\nScope of Transitional CNR Group Study, continued\n\n##### Tariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP Section 5.1.1.3\n\nInterconnection requests submitted to Interconnecting Transmission Owners or their distribution company a",

Chang.\n\nISO New England Inc. Eversource Energy Service Company\n\nDocket No. ER24-2009-001\n\nORDER ON COMPLIANCE AND TARIFF REVISIONS\n\n(Issued August 28, 2025)\n\nOn June 3, 2025, ISO New England Inc. (ISO-NE) and the Participating Transmission Owners Administrative Committee (PTO AC) on behalf of the New England Participating Transmission Owners (PTO) 1 (together, Filing Parties) submitted proposed revisions (Second Compliance Filing) to ISO-NE’s Transmission, Markets and Services Tariff (Tariff) 2 in compliance with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A 3 and the Commission’s order on compliance issued on April 4, 2025. 4 As discussed below, we find that Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing complies with the requirements of\n\n1 See infra app. B (listing PTOs).\n\n2 Section II of the Tariff contains the ISO-NE Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT). Schedule 11 of the OATT governs cost allocation for Generating Facility and Elective Transmission Upgrade interconnection-related upgrades. Schedule 22 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Large Generator Interconnection Agreement (LGIA). Schedule 23 of the OATT contains the ISO-NE Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) and ISO-NE pro forma Small Generator Interconnection Agreement (SGIA). Attachment K to the OATT governs the Regional System Planning Process. Section III of the Tariff contains the Market Rules. Capitalized terms that are not defined in this order have the meaning specified in Tariff, section I.2 (Rules of Construction; Definitions).\n\n3Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procs. & Agreements, Order No. 2023, 184 FERC ¶ 61,054, order on reh’g, 185 FERC ¶ 61,063 (2023), order on reh’g, Order No. 2023-A, 186 FERC ¶ 61,199, errata notice, 188 FERC ¶ 61,134 (2024).\n\n4ISO New England Inc., 191 FERC ¶ 61,018 (2025) (April Compliance Order).\n\nOrder Nos. 2023 and 2023-A and with the Commission’s directives in the April Compliance Order. Accordingly, we accept the Second Compliance Filing, effective August 12, 2024, as requested. 5\n\n## I. Background\n\nOrder Nos. 2023 and 2023-A require all public utility transmission providers to adopt revised pro forma LGIPs, pro forma LGIAs, pro forma SGIPs, and pro forma SGIAs. 6 These revisions ensure that interconnection customers are able to interconnect to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner, and will prevent undue discrimination. 7 In Order No. 2023, the Commission adopted a comprehensive package of reforms in three general categories: (1) reforms to implement a first-ready, first-served cluster study process; (2) reforms to increase the speed of interconnection queue processing; and (3) reforms to incorporate technological advancements into the interconnection process.\n\nOn May 14, 2024, in Docket No. ER24-2009-000, the Filing Parties 8 in that case submitted proposed revisions to the ISO-NE Tariff to comply with the requirements of Order No. 2023 (First Compliance Filing). 9 In the April Compliance Order, the Commission found that Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing partially complied with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. Accordingly, the Commission\n\n5 See infra app. A (providing eTariff records).\n\n6 The pro forma LGIP and pro forma LGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities larger than 20 MW. The pro forma SGIP and pro forma SGIA establish the terms and conditions under which public utilities that own, control, or operate facilities for transmitting energy in interstate commerce must provide interconnection service to generating facilities no larger than 20 MW. Order No. 2023, 184 FERC ¶ 61,054 at P 2.\n\n7Id. P 1.\n\n8 The filing parties that submitted the First Compliance Filing in Docket No. ER24-2009-000 also included the New England Power Pool (NEPOOL). Noting this difference, for simplicity this order refers to the filing parties in either proceeding as “Filing Parties.”\n\n9 Filing Parties also submitted a companion filing consisting of revisions not directly required by Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A in Docket No. ER24-2007-000. The April Compliance Order addressed and accepted the proposed revisions submitted in Docket No. ER24-2007-000.\n\nDocket No. ER24-2009-001 - 3 accepted Filing Parties’ First Compliance Filing in part, effective August 12, 2024, and directed the Filing Parties to submit a further compliance filing within 60 days of the issuance of the April Compliance Order. Specifically, the Commission directed further revisions to ISO-NE’s Tariff to comply with the following requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A: cluster study process, allocation of cluster network upgrade costs, site control, commercial readiness, LGIA deposit, transition process, elimination of reasonable efforts, affected system study process and modeling requirements, affected system pro forma agreements, and modeling and ride-through requirements for non-synchronous generating facilities. 10\n\nII. Second Compliance Filing\n\nFiling Parties state that through the Second Compliance Filing they propose revisions to ISO-NE Tariff section I.2.2, Tariff Schedules 11, 22 (ISO-NE LGIP), 23 (ISO-NE SGIP), and Tariff section III.13 in compliance with the April Compliance Order. Filing Parties also state that they have made conforming changes to the ISO-NE SGIP and Tariff Schedule 25 (Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures) 11 consistent with the directives of the April Compliance Order.\n\n10 April Compliance Order, 191 FERC ¶ 61,018 at PP 77, 85, 100, 102, 133, 135, 152-153, 158, 186, 197, 218, 227, 280-283.\n\n11 Schedule 25 contains interconnection requirements for Elective Transmission Upgrades. The Tariff defines an “Elective Transmission Upgrade” as follows:\n\nElective Transmission Upgrade . . . shall mean a new Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is interconnecting to the Administered Transmission System, or an upgrade to an existing Pool Transmission Facility, Merchant Transmission Facility or Other Transmission Facility that is part of or interconnected to the Administered Transmission System for which the Interconnection Customer has agreed to pay all of the costs of said Elective Transmission Upgrade and of any additions or modifications to the Administered Transmission System that are required to accommodate the Elective Transmission Upgrade. An Elective Transmission Upgrade is not a Generator Interconnection Related Upgrade, a Regional Transmission Upgrade, or a Market Efficiency Transmission Upgrade.\n\nTariff, § II, Schedule 25 (12.0.0), § 1 (Definitions), Elective Transmission Upgrade.\n\nFiling Parties request that the proposed Tariff revisions become effective on August 12, 2024. 12\n\nIII. Notice of Filing and Responsive Pleadings\n\nNotice of Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing was published in the Federal Register, 90 Fed. Reg. 24275 (June 9, 2025), with comments, interventions, and protests due on or before June 24, 2025. NEPOOL filed supportive comments requesting that the Commission accept the Second Compliance Filing without delay, condition, or modification. 13\n\n## IV. Discussion\n\nAs discussed below, we find that Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing complies with the requirements of Order Nos. 2023 and 2023-A. Accordingly, we accept Filing Parties’ Second Compliance Filing, effective August 12, 2024, as requested.\n\nWe find that Filing Parties have complied with the following directives from the April Compliance Order by largely proposing to adopt without modification the pro forma LGIP, pro forma LGIA, pro forma SGI",

steps.content:
I now have comprehensive information to provide a thorough answer. Let me compile the complete response covering all the requirements and fees for submitting a valid large generator interconnection application in ISO New England.

# Requirements and Fees for a Valid Large Generator Interconnection Application in ISO New England

Based on the latest **ISO New England Interconnection Process Guide** and **Schedule 22 – Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)** of the ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT), a "large generator" generally refers to a generating facility **larger than 20 MW** interconnecting to the Administered Transmission System (Pool Transmission Facilities/PTF and Non-PTF). The LGIP applies to all Large Generating Facilities, except as expressly provided. Below is a consolidated overview of the requirements and fees to submit a valid Interconnection Request (IR) under the post-Order No. 2023 framework now in effect.

## 1. Eligibility and Path to the Application
Before submitting, the developer must confirm that the ISO-NE interconnection process (not a state process) applies, and obtain:
- An **ISO Customer ID** (via the "Request Generation Interconnect Customer ID" form submitted to memcoord@iso-ne.com) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide, 2026-05 filing window context].
- **Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance** for IR data.
- An **IRTT (Interconnection Request Tracking Tool) account**.
- Confirmation of the interconnection path (PTF vs. Non-PTF) and the interconnecting transmission owner (ITO) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; Interconnection Process Presentation, Feb 16, 2023].

The LGIP itself (Section 2.1) states that "the LGIP and LGIA shall apply to Interconnection Requests pertaining to Large Generating Facilities."

## 2. Timing: Submissions Only During a Cluster Request Window
Per FERC Order No. 2023, the LGIP operates on a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**. Applications are accepted **only during an open Cluster Request Window (CRW)**, expected to last **45 calendar days**. The next window was posted as **October 5, 2026 – November 19, 2026** (for the next Cluster Study) [source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; ER24-2009-001 (Aug 28, 2025)].

## 3. Required Contents of a Valid Interconnection Request (Section 3.4)
The Appendix 1 IR form (the official Interconnection Request form under the LGIP) must include the following items during the CRW, as specified in Section 3.4.2 and the Appendix 1 form [source: Appendix 1 to Schedule 22, ISO-NE; "Notice Regarding Regulatory Limitations and Site Control," June 2, 2025; ISO-NE Q&A on Order 2023]:

(a) **Completed Appendix 1 IR Form** – including:
  - Project information (name, type – new facility, uprate/Material Modification, market entry, or upgrade from NR to CNR).
  - Facility location (Town/City, County, State).
  - **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)** – a definitive POI is required.
  - Type of facility, fuel type, MW capacity (gross, net, and at the POI at multiple temperature points).
  - Whether the facility includes **electric storage**; if so, charging capability, aggregate reactive capability, and primary frequency response operating range.
  - General equipment configuration (# of units, GSUs, control technologies).
  - Requested **Commercial Operation Date**, **Initial Synchronization Date**, and **In-Service Date**.
  - Customer information and ISO Customer ID.

(b) **Type of Interconnection Service elected (one only)**:
  - **Network Resource Interconnection Service (NR/NI)** – energy only, OR
  - **Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR/CNI)** – energy and capacity.

(c) **Technical data and models** (per the IR Technical Data Submittal Guidance, Rev. 1.0 dated 07/21/2025), including:
  - PSS®E steady-state (.idv/.raw) and dynamic (.dyr/.snp) models.
  - Short-circuit data for ASPEN (.olr/.chf) with positive- and zero-sequence impedances.
  - PSCAD models (.pscx/.pswx) for inverter-based resources (compatible with Intel Fortran 12 or later, ≥10–20 μs time step).
  - PSS®E–PSCAD benchmarking report.
  - Project one-line diagram (stamped by a PE if > 50 kW).
  - PQ (D) curve (0.95 leading/lagging reactive capability).
  - Collection system detail impedance sheet.
  - PRC-024-2 or ISO-NE SRD voltage/frequency protection settings (Attachment A-1).
  - Generation/transformer data, Attachment A (supplementary wind/inverter data), Attachment A-1 (protection settings), and any Attachment 2 (Surplus Interconnection) materials if applicable [source: Interconnection Request Submission Job Aid, 1/12/2022; Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance, 7/21/2025].

(d) **Site Control** (Section 3.4.2) – the most significant non-financial eligibility hurdle:
  - **For CNR/CNI Service: 100% exclusive Site Control is required** – no exception.
  - **For NR/NI Service: 100% exclusive Site Control is required**, **UNLESS** a Regulatory Limitation applies. If so, the customer must submit:
    - A **$10,000/MW cash deposit** (minimum $500,000, maximum $2,000,000),
    - A signed affidavit from a corporate officer that Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations, and
    - Documentation sufficiently describing the source and effects of the Regulatory Limitation.
  - The customer must demonstrate 100% Site Control within 180 calendar days of the effective date of the executed Interconnection Agreement [source: Appendix 1 to Schedule 22; ISO-NE Interconnection Technical Notice, "Providing Proof of Regulatory Limitations As a Demonstration of Site Control," 6/2/2025].
  - Existing facility modifications (no additional real property) may self-certify Site Control.

(e) **Initial Deposit** – **$50,000** (potentially non-refundable), applied to ISO-NE costs of processing the IR, the study agreements and attachments, and the LGIA [source: LGIP Section 3.4.1.1, as finalized in the 5/3/2025 effective date filing (ER25-2149-000) and the January 23, 2024 NEPOOL TC presentation].

(f) **Study Deposit** – **$250,000** for a new request for NR or CNR Interconnection Service, or **$100,000** for a request to change from existing NR to CNR Interconnection Service. This deposit is applied toward the cost of the Cluster Study [source: LGIP Section 3.4.1.1].

(g) **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** – equal to **two times the study deposit** (i.e., **$500,000** for new NR/CNR IRs, or **$200,000** for NR-to-CNR upgrades), provided as either (i) cash by electronic transfer, (ii) an irrevocable Letter of Credit from an approved financial institution (per the "List of Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers" on ISO-NE's public website), or (iii) a combination. (Per ISO-NE's December 21, 2023 TC summary, the Surety Bond Form is also accepted per the Order 2023 Key Project page; sureties must be on the List of Approved Surety Issuers.) This deposit may be refunded/credited per Section 3.7 depending on whether the customer advances or withdraws [source: LGIP Section 3.4.2(vi); LGIP Section 3.1 (Letter of Credit provisions); ISO-NE Order 2023 Key Project page – CRD Instructions/Forms].

The ISO must validate or notify of deficiencies within **5 business days** of receipt; the customer then has up to **10 business days** to cure (but no later than CRW close) [source: ISO-NE New Process FAQ, 1/18/2024, Q3; LGIP Section 3.4.4].

## 4. Post-Acceptance Financial Milestones (Beyond the IR Submission)
The fees above are those to submit a *valid* application. Additional financial commitments come into play during the cluster study cycle and beyond (these are part of the overall "requirements to remain in the process" but generally not part of the original IR submission package):

- **Additional CRD after Cluster Study Report**: an additional CRD equal to **5% of the customer's share of Network Upgrade costs** is due within 20 calendar days after the Cluster Study Report meeting.
- **Interconnection Facilities Study (Section 8)**: To enter the Facilities Study, a Commercial Readiness Deposit equal to the **greater of 25% of the estimated Facilities Study cost or $250,000** is required.
- **LGIA deposit (Section 11.3)**: Upon execution of the LGIA, an LGIA deposit is required (20% of Network Upgrade cost less prior readiness deposits on hand) [source: ISO-NE October 17, 2023 TC presentation, "Additional Information on the Cluster Study Cycle"; Appendix 3 to Schedule 22 (Facilities Study Agreement); IR Submittal guidance].

## 5. Other Submission Logistics
- **Form of IR**: Submit via IRTT during the CRW.
- **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)**: Within 15 business days after CRW close, ISO issues NDAs; all ICs must execute them before the Cluster Study Scoping Meeting.
- **Cluster Study Agreement (Appendix 2)**: Issued by ISO within 5 business days after CRW close; must be executed (along with all required technical data) prior to the close of the 60-calendar-day Customer Engagement Window.
- **Withdrawal penalties** apply per Section 3.7; deposits may be forfeited or refunded depending on when and why the customer withdraws [source: LGIP Section 3.7; ISO-NE Order 2023 implementation presentations].

## Summary of Fees to Submit a Valid Large Generator IR
| Fee / Deposit | Amount | Refundability | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial (Application) Deposit | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | ISO-NE costs of processing the IR, study agreements, LGIA development |
| Study Deposit | **$250,000** (new NR/CNR); $100,000 (NR→CNR change) | Refundable; applied to Cluster Study | Cluster Study costs |
| Commercial Readiness Deposit | **$500,000** (new; = 2× study deposit); $200,000 (NR→CNR change) | Refundable/creditable per Section 3.7; may be cash, LOC, or Surety Bond | Demonstrates commercial commitment |
| Site Control (NR only, with Regulatory Limitation) | **$10,000/MW** cash (min $500,000 / max $2,000,000) | Refundable upon demonstrated 100% Site Control | In lieu of Site Control for NR service when a Regulatory Limitation applies |
| Site Control (CNR) | 100% exclusive Site Control required | n/a | Eligibility – no deposit alternative |
